content.problemStatement:
Hackerman wants to know who is the better player between Bob and Alice with the help of a game.

The game proceeds as follows:


First, Alice throws a die and gets the number AAA
Then, Bob throws a die and gets the number BBB
Alice wins the game if the sum on the dice is a prime number; and Bob wins otherwise.
Given AAA and BBB, determine who wins the game.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
The first and only line of each test case contains two space-separated integers AAA and BBB.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the winner of the game: `Alice` or `Bob`.


Each letter of the output may be printed in either uppercase or lowercase, i.e, `Alice`, `ALICE`, `AlIce` and `aLIcE` will all be considered equivalent.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains two space‑separated integers A and B (the results of Alice's and Bob's dice throws).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing either "Alice" if A+B is a prime number, or "Bob" otherwise.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: 2+1=3 (prime) → Alice.\nTest case 2: 1+1=2 (prime) → Alice.\nTest case 3: 2+2=4 (not prime) → Bob.
Sums are 3,7,12,11 respectively. 3,7,11 are prime → Alice, 12 is not → Bob.
Sums are 2 and 11, both prime, so Alice wins both games.

solution.approach: For each test case compute S = A + B. Since the maximum possible sum is 12, we can pre‑store the set of prime numbers up to 12: {2,3,5,7,11}. If S belongs to this set Alice wins, otherwise Bob wins. This yields O(1) time per test case and O(1) extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work for each of the T test cases.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few integer variables and a tiny lookup table.
